evals.name: cohort-retention-chart-choice
evals.prompt: I'm a data engineer at a SaaS company visualizing churn for our finance team's monthly board deck. I have 24 monthly signup cohorts × 12 observation months — it's triangular because cohort N can only be observed for 12-N months. I was going to do a heatmap but our designer wants 'something more polished'. What chart should I use?
evals.expected_output: Defends the heatmap (or a small-multiples retention curve) on perceptual grounds; explains why polish ≠ decoration; cites Bertin (matrix/value encoding) or Tufte (small multiples) reasoning; concrete recommendation.

assertions.text: Defends the heatmap or recommends small-multiples cohort retention curves (not a 3D surface, not a pie chart, not a single averaged line)
Explicitly addresses how to handle the triangular shape / missing cells (greyed out, NaN, diagonal cutoff)
Reframes 'polish' as data presentation craft (typography, spacing, color scale) rather than chartjunk/decoration
Specifies a sensible color scale for retention — sequential or diverging anchored on a target, NOT rainbow
Provides at least one concrete actionable next step (revised chart description, code, or design spec)

evals.name: 3d-bar-rainbow-critique
evals.prompt:
Review this matplotlib code for our weekly active users chart on the exec dashboard. It's a 3D bar chart with weeks on X, product (Web/iOS/Android) on Y, and DAU on Z, with a rainbow colormap. Any problems?

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

weeks = np.arange(13)
products = ['Web', 'iOS', 'Android']
xs, ys = np.meshgrid(weeks, range(3))
ax.bar3d(xs.flatten(), ys.flatten(), 0,
         0.8, 0.8, dau.flatten(), cmap='rainbow')
ax.set_xlabel('WEEK')
ax.set_ylabel('PRODUCT')
ax.set_zlabel('DAILY ACTIVE USERS')
ax.set_title('WEEKLY ACTIVE USERS')
plt.show()
```
evals.expected_output: Identifies multiple Tufte violations: 3-D occlusion / Lie Factor, rainbow colormap not perceptually uniform and not ordered, all-caps labels, vertical Z-axis label. Recommends flattening to small-multiple line/bar chart by product or a single line chart with 3 series labeled at line ends. Provides revised code or a clear redesign sketch.

assertions.text: Identifies the 3D projection as a problem (occlusion, perspective distortion, or hard to read)
Identifies the rainbow colormap as a problem (not perceptually uniform, not ordered, or confusing)
Identifies the all-caps labels as a stylistic issue
Recommends flattening to 2D: small multiples by product OR single 2D chart with 3 labeled series OR grouped bars
Provides revised code OR a concrete, code-equivalent redesign description

evals.name: choropleth-jenks-vs-quantiles
evals.prompt: I have COVID case rate per 100k people per US county for 2020-2023, and I want to make a choropleth map for an article showing 'America's hardest-hit counties'. Should I use Jenks natural breaks or quantiles for the color scale? My intuition is Jenks but my editor says quantiles look more 'honest'. Which is right?
evals.expected_output: Addresses the Jenks-vs-quantiles tradeoff substantively, but also raises the Bertin critique that choropleths with pre-classed intervals convert Q into O — recommending proportional symbols / dot density as an alternative for discovery, while accepting choropleth for *communication*. Honest about which class scheme suits the communication goal (probably Jenks for natural clusters when communicating a known story; quantiles when discovering or comparing across maps). Mentions log scale if the case-rate distribution is highly skewed.

assertions.text: Gives a decisive recommendation between Jenks and quantiles (not just 'it depends')
Explains what each scheme does perceptually and how it shapes the story the map tells
Raises at least one alternative to a pre-classed choropleth (proportional symbols, dot density, dasymetric, bivariate, or hex bins) OR notes the loss of quantitative resolution when pre-classing
Addresses the skewed distribution of county case rates (log scale, outlier handling, or trimming the legend)
Notes the communication-vs-discovery framing (for an *article*, the goal is communication, which sometimes warrants Jenks's natural-break narrative)

evals.name: cs-dashboard-at-risk-accounts
evals.prompt: I'm building a new Looker dashboard for our customer success team. They need to spot at-risk accounts quickly. We have ~500 active accounts, and for each I have: MRR per account, last login date, NPS score (last 90 days), open support tickets count, contract renewal date. How should I lay this out?
evals.expected_output: Treats this as a Bertin 'processing' problem (the user is hunting anomalies, not communicating a known result). Recommends a sortable/filterable table-as-primary-view with sparklines or in-line bars per account, NOT a wall of separate charts (gauges, donuts, etc.). Identifies the right at-risk signals (recent low NPS + ticket spike + low usage + near renewal). Considers a small-multiple scatter (e.g., MRR × login recency colored by NPS) as a secondary discovery view. Specifically discourages KPI tiles with bare numbers.

assertions.text: Recommends a table-as-primary-view (sortable rows = accounts) rather than a wall of separate KPI tiles or gauges
Includes in-cell visual elements (sparklines, inline bars, dot-plots, or color encoding within the table) rather than separate charts
Identifies concrete at-risk signals to compute (e.g., NPS drop, ticket spike, login dormancy, near renewal date) — not just 'show the data'
Optionally proposes a secondary scatter or small-multiple view for discovery (e.g., MRR × login recency, ticket spike heatmap)
Discourages bare KPI tiles, gauges, donuts, or 3D bars
